sentences.text: Well, my father gave her money to do it.
He paid her.
No.
This is something that's been told to me, yes.
Yes.
Well, not exactly.
But what happened was, one day, she said to me we're going into the city to Cracow to meet Victor.
That was the boyfriend.
And so we, we went in.
And she took me to a church.
And she took me in and said, you sit here and wait for me.
And she went across the street to a cafe.
This was on [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's where she was to meet him.
So I sat in the church, and I waited and waited and waited for hours.
And she didn't come back for me.
She didn't return.
So I guess, eventually, I just walked out of the church.
And the whole street was cordoned off.
And the cafe had been raided by the Gestapo.
I-- at the time, I didn't know.
You know, I was just seven years old, almost eight.
But I didn't know.
But I-- since then, I've learned that this was a cafe that was frequented by the underground.
That's, that's where they used to meet.
In fact, that particular arrest was a very famous arrest.
And it's been written up in a book, [INAUDIBLE], the Polish book.
There were just so many people there that were arrested.
It was like a major underground meeting.
I had thought that he was.
But when I returned to Poland in recent years, I met Victor's brother.
And he said no.
He was alerted not to go.
So he didn't.
He wasn't there.
So she was arrested.
I don't know for sure.
I really don't know.
I think she was sent to concentration camp.
I do not know.
I think she survived.
But I'm not sure if she did or not.
No, he did not.
He-- I don't have any negative feelings toward him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't have any recollection, actually.
I mean, I remember what he looked like.
But I don't remember him really dealing too much with me because when he came, I had to stay away.
So there I was in the street, walking the street, crying.
It was, it was getting dark-- and crying.
And all of a sudden, a woman came up.
And she was wearing a black cape.
She stuck me under the cape.
And she pulled me away and took me into the building where the cafe was.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she took me upstairs to the, I think, fourth or fifth floor of the apartment building.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The apartment belonged to the Alicia Golomp.
The woman who took me off the street was this sort of a superintendent or concierge of the building.
And she, she took me upstairs.
Alicia and-- her husband's name was Ludwig.
Alicia asked me, you know, who-- you know, what's your name, and who are you, and what's happened to you?
And I had been told to, to-- I had this pat story that was sort of drilled into me.
And the story was that I'm the daughter of a Polish officer from Warsaw and that my parents were killed in a bombing raid.
And I was just downstairs with my, my cousin, and she was arrested.
And, and so Alicia said, OK, so you, you know, stay here.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It, it was customary that if the Gestapo came, they would return.
That was their mode of operation.
So they had to get me out of there, they felt.
Also--
Well, they had-- they were in the building also.
See, I've learned this since, that in the building was another-- there was a, a SS man living.
Also, Alicia's mother was very active in the underground.
She had a-- she ran a hospital in her apartment.
They had shortwave radios.
They had ammunition.
So they got scared.
And they-- that same night or just a few hours, or an hour after, the, the oldest son, Stasczek, took me, and we walked for several kilometers to a farm outside of the city.
This was a, a farm that was owned by the Catholic church and administered by Ludwig's brother, Jan.
And I stayed on the farm.
That's where I remained--
--for the most part-- for the most of the war, the rest of the war.
Well, I've asked them since.
And they said to me, we suspected it, but we didn't really want to know because if you were, it was more dangerous for them to know.
The other thing I neglected to say is that the way I looked had really everything to do with my survival because I was so fair.
I was-- my hair was very, very, very light.
And I looked like a little Aryan child, Polish child, Swedish child but not, you know, [INAUDIBLE].
I wasn't dark.
And so I remained on the farm.
And they didn't ask me any more questions.
But they didn't-- they were afraid to send me to school.
They said it was dangerous for them because they didn't-- they had to explain my presence.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but they were very kind.
They cared a lot about me.
Alicia had a family of eight children.
The youngest were twins.
They were 15 and 16-- they were 15 or 16 years old.
Just neighbors' children.
Yes.
The neighbors' children I played with.
But it was, it was-- I remember being very contented and happy playing in the barn, barn.
You know, jumping in the hay and taking milk to wherever they took the milk, you know, in big vats, you know, milking cows.
And there was a big fruit orchard there.
And climbing trees and things like that.
And one thing that happened was that my cousin's father, Oscar--
This-- the cousins that took take care of me.
Oscar Singer had heard that his daughter was arrested.
And he heard where I was because he, he, he inquired.
I mean, he came back.
And he looked for me.
And, and so he came to the farm to get me, probably because he was afraid that I would say something.
And I didn't want to go for good reasons.
And the family said, look, she's fine.
Just leave her.
So he left me.
But they were always a little confused because a package was sent with some of my clothing.
And a lot of my clothing and, like, rubber boots were-- came from Russia.
So they didn't quite understand what, what that was all about.
So I remained on the farm.
Right.
There, there was Jan.
And Eugenia was his wife.
Jan was the brother of Ludwig.
So they were the older couple.
Then there was, there was Stasczek.
Jan was Ludwig's brother.
Alicia was the wife, Ludwig's wife.
And she was the mother of the eight children.
They were mostly grown up.
And they were, they were-- some of them were there.
Some were, were not.
Some were in the underground.
And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] was Eugenia's and Jan's daughter.
So she was the-- she was in her 20s at time.
And so she, she basically kind of took care of me.
And Eugenia took care of me.
I was the youngest.
I was a child there.
And then, one time, I remember we used to make or slaughter, you know, a pig or calf.
We weren't allowed to keep the meat.
And--
Well, the-- they were not allowed to.
They-- all the meat had to be sent out to the, to the Germans.
But one time we slaughtered a, a pig.
And it was a very messy affair because they, they, you know, stuffed the sausages, and they cut up all this stuff.
And we were alerted that the Gestapo was coming.
So we had to hide everything.
And now the story goes-- and I don't remember doing this, but the Polish family told me this-- when the Gestapo came, I started to sing and dance.
And they start-- they-- and, and I said, I said, get, get the vodka.
You know, get the-- give them vodka.
So they gave them vodka.
And I'm singing, dancing, and they just laughed.
And they were amused.
And they left.
I have no recollection of doing this at all.
So I was, basically-- you know, I didn't go to school.

And the family has said to me since-- you know, as I said, I've met, I have seen them twice since, since the end of the war-- that, you know, that the people wanted to know who I was and even the mayor of the town, and-- so there was a church.
I used to go to church.
The family was pretty religious.
We used to go to church.
I knew my prayers.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But I always knew I was Jewish, somehow.
I wondered where, where everybody was.
I wondered.
But I, I knew that I had to keep this a secret that I was Jewish.
I knew that I had-- couldn't tell anything.
I, I knew all this, you know.
Keeping this secret--
--this tremendous secret.
I don't know.
I don't know.
It's, it's, it's-- I've tried very hard to, you know, to get in touch with all that.
And I-- it's-- I, I really can't.
Yeah.
Couple years.
A couple years.
I do.
I do because I was well cared for.
Stasczek has told, had told me that when I came, you know, I had nothing.
I had no clothes, nothing, you know.
So they had to go out and buy me clothes.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they were very good to me.
I don't remember reading at all.
I don't remember writing at all.
[LAUGHS]
But I was just going to add--
--that Stasczek used to do the bookkeeping, you know, the accounting for the farm.
And he said I was very good with numbers, which I'm not [LAUGHS] now, but-- and I used to help him, he said.
And they also said that the farm used to send-- they used to send some food to the ghetto.
I mean, secretly send it.
I do because what I remember was the bombing-- being in-- having to go to the, into the shelter in the middle of the night.
And I remember the-- I remember the bombing.
And to this day, certain sounds scare me terribly.
We would go in the cellar and wait till the siren, you know, sounded when it was over.
So, so I remember going back there.
I don't know how-- what-- for what reason.
But I used to-- we used to go back.
And so when did this come to an end, your stay on the farm?
Well, the Russians liberated us.
And I, I remember the fighting when, when they were coming because there was, there was fighting, you know.
There were-- I remember going to the outhouse and my-- and, the, you know, like, remember bullets going by.
So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Russians liberated us.
And then, my uncle, my cousin, his father, came back one more time.
Oscar.
Oscar came back for me.
And at that point, the family felt that they didn't have a right to keep me.
And they turned me over to him.
I didn't want to go, but they did.
No, I don't think I was aware.
I, I don't think I understood what that really meant.
I mean, I think I knew it was something good.
But I don't-- I didn't really know the implications of it.
In 1945, I was 10 years old, yes.
Oscar came and took me away.
And I went to live with him and his wife.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that point, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oscar and his wife still continued to live on their Polish papers.
And at that point, they, they had me confirmed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and sent me to church to study.
And I, I was confirmed.
No, I did not start going to school.
But I remember going to the catechism, you know, to learn to be confirmed.
And I stayed with them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I wasn't, I wasn't permitted to visit the Polish family.
And--
They wouldn't allow me to go.
And one time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one time, the, the family saw me in the park.
And they, you know, they felt very upset and hurt and, and really missed me terribly.
But they wouldn't-- my, my cousin would not have anything to do with them.
And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and the, the story also goes that I-- one time when I was in the park by myself, I, I went to visit them because-- on my own.
But I wasn't allowed to go.
It, it was within walking distance.
I don't recall any beatings.
But I don't have-- I don't feel very warmly toward-- to him or his wife.
And shortly after I was with them, he turned me over to the refugee center at the Jewish committee.
He brought me over there and just left me there.
So that's, that's where I was.
Maybe for his own safety.
You know, for-- maybe he, he was fearful for his-- that he wouldn't be known as a Jew.
And he didn't know what I would say or do or--
That was in Cracow on [NON-ENGLISH].
And that's where everybody returned.
That was the central place where everybody came back.
I was the-- I was one of the children that was brought back.
I was very sick at that point because I remember being in the infirmary.
I had jaundice.
And there were a whole bunch of kids who were sick.
And I remained there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until a, a woman-- her name was Lena Kuchler-- came.
And she saw 50, 60, children just in one room without any clothing, adequate care.
Some were sick.
Some were terribly frightened.
And she who, herself, lost her own family, made a decision that she had to do something for these children.
So she, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somehow, managed to get a home.
And she had two homes for the children.
One was in Zakopane.
Zakopane was a resort town in the Carpathian Mountains, maybe a couple hours away from Cracow.
And so she rented this big home.
And the other home was in Rabka.
In Rabka, the children who went there were children who had tuberculosis.
And while we were still in, in the refugee center, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- the place was stoned, and it was very unsafe because some of the children were being sent to school.
And they were, they were not accepted.
There was tremendous anti-Semitism.
And, and so I remember the day that we were being transported to, to Zakopane in the trucks.
And we had to leave quickly and without much-- not to be noticed, you know.
And then the tarp was pulled over so we wouldn't be seen.
And we were taken over there.
In Zakopane?
[SIGHS] It's hard to know.
I don't know.
Maybe months.
I'm not sure.
Well, we went on outings.
We, we, we had-- we were in different age groups.
And we slept in a dormitory.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And every child was given a, a little bag of powdered milk because [LAUGHS] I remember keeping it in a little night table.
I remember keeping it there.
And I used to eat it dry with the end of my [LAUGHS] toothbrush.
I remember doing that.
But it was-- the counselors took care of us.
And we used to have fun, except that I had-- I don't know whether I had rickets or something, but I had trouble going on-- walking a lot.
My, my joints were swollen, and I-- so I had trouble going on long trips.
Some of the children went to school, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but they had a lot of bad experiences.
Beatings.
Stonings.
And--
No, I did not go to school.
And one time, men came on horseback with guns.
The older children, the teenagers, were equipped with guns, ammunition, and they defended the home.
And they-- and the, you know, the attackers were-- went away.
So, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so-- but one day, when Lena-- we called Lena matka, mother.
And, and we, we felt like she was our mother.
You know, my, my, my nickname was Krysza.
Oh, now I remember, [LAUGHS] Krysza.
And one day-- What does that mean?
It's diminutive for Krystina, Krysza.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But one day, Lena went into town.
I had written a letter to Oscar Singer.
And she brought the letter to the refugee center.
At that same time, my father, who had survived and was looking for me, came.
And he saw the letter because he saw the name.
And that's how he found me.
And Lena came and said, I have this wonderful news for you.
Your father has survived, and he'll be here now.
So-- and my father finally came.
I do.
I-- she took me-- Lena took me up to her office.
And my father came.
And when I first saw my father, I was absolutely terrified of him.
He looked like a skeleton.
I was terribly frightened.
I didn't want this strange person hugging me and kissing me.
And, and I, I was just very uncomfortable with it.
And I think, I think I realized it also meant change, another change for me.
You know, I had already made so many changes.
And this time, I had a mother, and I had all these friends.
And I was ready to stay.
So--
OK.
